availability.p:
Published under a
Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 48 [Bnf 12926 Translation]
div.p:
This truth is further corroborated: for the Church not only had temporal power in the time of Sylvester, but obtained greater strength in the time of Pope Gregory, in whose age Maurice and Phocas, pious men for some time and the greatest Caesars, possessed the Roman empire. Nevertheless, Gregory never dealt with them so that they might wage war against the pagans who were not doing injury to the Church, even if Maurice was the spiritual godfather of Pope Gregory, and therefore it is probable that there was a not uncommon friendship between them. This is clear first because if Saint Gregory had incited them to such a war, the matter would have been worthy of memory and would be recounted by the writers of that time.
Secondly, because Gregory, in order to lead the English people to the faith, by no means sent armed forces against them, but a certain monk Augustine and forty pious monks distinguished by Christian integrity, who before they entered the city of England, beseeched the Lord with these words: "O Lord, who created and redeemed these souls, deign to take away Your anger from them, so that with us together mercy and clemency may enter to them." Thus it is read in the life of blessed Gregory written by Paul the Deacon and in
the history of the Lombards, book 3, chapter 12. And concerning this prayer and the preaching of those holy monks, the venerable Bede writes at length in the history which he wrote concerning the peoples of that island. Those holy monks proceeded, and having performed many miracles which led Gregory himself into admiration, as he himself wrote to Eulogius, Bishop of Alexandria, in book [gap], letter [gap], and to Augustine himself in book 9, letter 58.

Do you see that the Church, even in the time when it flourished with the greatest strength, used not material but spiritual arms in the preaching of the gospel, in no way departing from the example and instruction of its teacher? Again, in the days of that same blessed Gregory, before he was assumed to the supreme pontificate, there were popes and emperors who were most Christian men, namely Pope John III
and Benedict I and Pelagius II, Roman pontiffs; nevertheless they did not induce Justin the Greater and Maurice, who were Roman caesars in their age, to overthrow idolatry by war. Then that most holy man Gregory himself, in homily 28 on the gospels, expounds those words of Christ "compel them to enter," written in the parable of the wedding feast, concerning the compulsion which is made by holy admonitions and examples of morals and Christian life and by the most efficacious force of the divine word.

And lest the error of these men be confounded by the doctrine of the sacred doctors alone, let there be added to the aforementioned exposition of the saints that which the professors of canon law understood, especially Innocent in c. maiores de baptismo, who speaks with these words: "and thus no one is to be compelled to become a Christian." Nor does it stand in the way that it is said to the servant that he compels those invited to the wedding to enter Luke 14 and 23 q. 4, c. displicet, because it is understood concerning compulsion made by the insistence of reason, not by the severity of the material sword or by temporal violence 45 distinctio, c. de iudaeis, because the execution of such a sword is forbidden to that servant, that is to the order of preachers or the apostles in the person of Peter by the Lord 23 q. 1 c. Item cum Petro. These are the words of Innocent.

Therefore, the servant who invites to the wedding, that is, the preacher of the gospel, should not be armed with bombards like a wolf among sheep to kill and plunder,
but instructed with holy morals and the word of God, he ought to announce the gospel, exposing his life to death for the extension of the gospel and the glory of Christ. For then the preaching of the gospel will be efficacious when preachers undergo death, not when they have killed others and affected them with death, according to Chrysostom on Matthew chapter 22, homily 41 in the imperfect work.

The prophets and apostles, in announcing the gospel, were despoiled of life; Christ also died for us all. Nor did the fruit of preaching appear before the death of Christ and the apostles. For just as no one eats the entrails of a living animal, so no one received the words of the living prophets and apostles, but after their death. Finally, just as the entrails of the lamb, so men did not eat the words of Christ until after he was slain. For after his death, the gospels were preached and believed, and in them men are daily nourished. So also the entrails of his prophets and apostles. These are from Chrysostom.

Which Christ himself, the eternal truth, wished to signify to us in John 12:
"Unless a grain of wheat falling into the earth dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it brings forth much fruit." But he speaks there of the preaching and conversion of the gentiles, according to
Saint Thomas in the third part, question 42, article 1 in the body. And Saint Augustine in his tract on John, expounding those words of Christ, says: he said he was the grain to be mortified in the unbelief of the Jews
and to be multiplied in the faith of all peoples. For that very death illuminated the teaching of the holy apostles and added faith to their words. Innocent also infers from these things: therefore it appears that war is not to be declared against the Saracens so that they become Christians. These are his words.

Therefore, neither is war to be undertaken as a means for preparing the minds of infidels or for removing idolatry. The gospel, therefore, is propagated not by lances but by the word of God and the Christian life, but by the insistence of reason, as Innocent has stated. Which Saint Gregory also teaches in book 11, letter 15, to Pascasius, Bishop of Naples, with these words: "Therefore it must be done that rather, provoked by reason and gentleness, they may wish to follow us, not flee." These are his words. Gregory speaks there about the Jews. But if the Jews, who are servants of the Church and prepared to serve, as in
c. dispar 23 q. 8 and in
c. etsi iudaeos: de iudaeis, are to be led to the faith by gentleness and doctrine, how much more must the same be taught concerning the pagans, who have been instructed by no words of God, and are not bound to depart from their own religion or princes before they see what is better.

Which
Scotus wisely confirms in the fourth book of the Sentences, distinction 3, question 4, saying:
"that no one is bound to any positive precept, even divine, unless it be promulgated to him by someone suitable and authentic,
or by a truthful form and testimony of good men to whom anyone ought reasonably to believe," etc. These are his words. Concerning which matter
Cajetan also learnedly discourses in 2 2ae, question 1, article 4, in the response to the second objection.

But what faith will be given to the Spaniards, rapacious, savage, and ferocious men, who slaughter the unarmed and harmless peoples of the Indians and plunder them with unheard-of avarice? Hence it follows that the Indians, seeing their impious works, do not sin nor are they worthy of punishment if they do not receive the gospel; nor would any people be culpable for not having received the faith of Christ, if the apostles, per impossibile, had been endowed with the morals with which the Spaniards are endowed. Concerning this we have the word of the Lord in John 15:
"If I had not done among them the works which no one else did, they would not have sin."

From what has been so extensively deduced, therefore, it clearly appears what or what kind is the literal sense which Christ understood in the aforementioned parable "Compel them to enter": namely, that the compulsion which must be understood there concerning unbelievers who have never heard the faith is that which is done by the insistence and human persuasion of reason, or by spiritual and interior means through the ministry of angels. And consequently it is false and impious to assert that Christ bound us by precept through that parable
that war against peaceful infidels should precede the preaching of the gospel when the Church had great strength, as the distinguished doctor Sepulveda has not blushed to put forth in a new doctrine.
